step.title: Skill Type and Architecture
step.goal: Determine skill type, identify what should be scripts, and plan the complete architecture. This step shapes everything about how the skill is built and used.
step.note:
THIS IS THE MOST IMPORTANT STEP. Key decisions made here: - Skill type (workflow/exec/data) - What should be scripts (SCRIPT-FIRST MENTALITY) - Reusable content plan (scripts, references, assets) - Overall structure
SCRIPT-FIRST PRINCIPLE: If anything CAN be a script, it probably SHOULD be a script rather than a prompt. Scripts are deterministic, token-efficient, and reliably repeatable.

substep.title: Analyze Requirements
substep.action:
Based on discovery, analyze the skill's nature: - Does it have multiple distinct phases? → workflow - Is it a quick lookup or transformation? → exec - Is it reference material for context? → data
Key questions: - Will users need to review/adjust intermediate outputs? - Does it produce a file incrementally or all at once? - Is there a "journey" the user goes through?

substep.title: Determine Type
substep.ask:
Based on what this skill does, which type fits best?
**workflow** - Multi-step, checkpoint-driven process - User interaction at each checkpoint - Produces output incrementally (template-output tags) - Examples: create-product-brief, agent-creator, deep-research
**exec** - Single-shot execution - Agent reads skill, performs action, returns result - No intermediate checkpoints - Examples: validate-yaml, format-output, check-dependencies
**data** - Reference material - Loaded on demand for context - Not "executed", just provides information - Examples: coding-standards, template-library, glossary

substep.title: Script-First Analysis
substep.action:
Apply script-first thinking. For each capability the skill needs, ask: - Is this deterministic and repeatable? → SCRIPT - Is it rewritten the same way every time? → SCRIPT - Does it need reliability over creativity? → SCRIPT - Is it a transformation, validation, or file operation? → SCRIPT
Only defer to prompts when the task genuinely requires: - Creative judgment - Context-dependent decisions - Natural language generation - User interaction
substep.ask:
What operations in this skill should be scripts?
Common script candidates: - Scaffolding (create files/directories) - Validation (check correctness) - Transformation (convert formats) - File operations (read/write/parse) - Calculations (deterministic logic)

check.action:
For each planned script, define:
| Field | Description | |-------|-------------| | name | Filename (kebab-case.py) | | purpose | What it does (one sentence) | | inputs | Arguments/parameters | | outputs | What it returns/creates | | dependencies | Python packages needed |
This design will be used to generate script templates in Step 6.
check.ask: Let's design each script. For each one, what are: - Name and purpose? - Inputs (arguments)? - Outputs (return value, files created)? - Dependencies (Python packages)?

check.action:
Confirm this skill genuinely needs no scripts.
This is valid for: - Pure data/reference skills - Skills that only orchestrate other skills - Skills where all work is cognitive/creative

substep.title: Plan Reusable Content
substep.action:
Categorize all reusable content:
| Resource | When to Use | Location | |----------|-------------|----------| | **scripts/** | Deterministic, repeatable code | references/scripts/ | | **references/** | Documentation loaded as needed | references/ | | **assets/** | Files used in output (not loaded) | assets/ |
Scripts: Already designed in 2d References: What documentation should be loadable? Assets: What files are used in output (templates, images)?
substep.ask: Beyond scripts, what other reusable content does this skill need? - Reference documentation? - Templates for output? - Static assets?

check.action: Outline the workflow steps (3-9 steps typical): - What's the natural progression? - Where are the checkpoints? - What does each step produce? - Which steps invoke scripts?
Define the execution pattern: - What input does it need? - What processing happens? (likely a script) - What output does it return?
Define the reference content: - What categories of information? - How should it be organized? - When would an agent load this?

substep.title: Present Architecture
substep.template-output:
Present the complete skill architecture:
**Type:** {{skill_type}}
**Scripts Planned:** (table of name, purpose, inputs, outputs per planned script)
**Reusable Content:** (per-skill references/ contents; cross-skill shared/ attachments if any)
**Structure:** (For workflow: list of steps with script references) (For exec: input → script → output) (For data: content outline)
**Files to Create:** - skill.yaml - head.md - skill-overview.md (workflow/exec) OR SKILL.md (data, flat layout) - references/scripts/*.py (if scripts planned) - references/steps/*.xml (if workflow type)
[a] Reconsider architecture [c] Continue [y] YOLO

section.title: Workflow Type
section.description: Multi-step, checkpoint-driven processes with user interaction. The user reviews and can adjust output at each checkpoint.

when-to-use.rule: Anything with "steps" the user should see and verify
When intermediate outputs need human judgment

section.structure: - instructions.xml with step references - references/steps/step-0N-*.xml for each step - template-output tags for checkpoints - 3-8 steps typical

section.title: Exec Type
section.description: Single-shot execution. Agent reads skill, performs action, returns result. No intermediate checkpoints or user interaction during execution.

when-to-use.rule: Operations that don't need human review mid-process

section.structure: - instructions.md (simpler, markdown-based) - No step files needed - Clear input/output specification

section.title: Data Type
section.description: Reference material loaded on demand. Not executed, just provides context. Agent reads this when it needs specific knowledge.
section.structure: - instructions.md with reference content - Organized by topic/category - No execution flow, just information

section.title: Choosing Between Types

rules.rule: If users need to review intermediate work → workflow
If it's "do this thing and tell me the result" → exec
If it's "here's information you might need" → data
When in doubt, consider: does this need checkpoints?

section.example: "Create a PRD" → workflow (user reviews each section) "Validate this YAML file" → exec (just return valid/invalid) "Follow these coding standards" → data (reference when coding)

section.title: Skills with Dual-Mode Callers
section.description:
A dual-mode primary (e.g., researcher) backs each menu item with a skill, and runs that skill both INTERACTIVELY (a human invoked the primary) and AUTONOMOUSLY (the primary was dispatched via Task, so the question tool is stripped). The same skill must serve both. Those skills are normal workflow skills — same skill.yaml + head.md + skill-overview.md + references/steps/ shape; the mode-awareness lives in the step content, not in a different structure.
The skill detects mode the same way the activation does — the question tool is available interactively, absent autonomously — then guards interactive-only constructs and provides an autonomous path for each.

rules.rule: Detect mode in an early step (question tool available → INTERACTIVE; absent → AUTONOMOUS)
Guard interactive-only constructs (<ask>, template-output checkpoints) behind <check if="INTERACTIVE">

For each interactive checkpoint, provide an autonomous path: parse the input from the dispatch prompt, and emit a structured return signal (SUCCESS / ERROR / CLARIFICATION_NEEDED / domain signals) via stdout
An autonomous caller cannot answer mid-dispatch; surface a needed answer as CLARIFICATION_NEEDED for re-dispatch via Task session resume, not as an <ask>
Minimum 3 steps: Setup → Work → Complete
Maximum 9 steps: Beyond this, consider splitting into multiple skills
Sweet spot: 5-7 steps
Each step should have a clear deliverable

section.title: Script-First Mentality
section.description: If anything CAN be a script, it probably SHOULD be a script. Scripts are deterministic, token-efficient, and reliably repeatable.

when-to-script.rule: Anything rewritten the same way repeatedly

section.example: "Create agent directory structure" → scaffold-agent.py "Validate YAML syntax" → validate.py "Parse and transform data" → transform.py
Writing a prompt to create directories (use script) Writing a prompt to validate JSON (use script)

section.title: Script Design Best Practices

rules.rule: Use PEP 723 inline metadata for dependencies
Include docstring with usage and output description
Use argparse for command-line arguments
Return meaningful exit codes (0 success, 1 error)
Print structured output (JSON for data, human-readable for status)

section.template:
# /// script # requires-python = ">=3.11" # dependencies = ["pyyaml>=6.0"] # /// """Brief description.
Usage: uv run script.py arg1 [arg2] Output: Description of what it produces """

section.title: Base Directory Convention
section.description: When a skill is loaded, the agent receives the skill's base directory path. All file references in SKILL.md should use {base_dir} placeholder.

rules.rule: Reference scripts as: uv run {base_dir}/scripts/name.py
Reference files as: {base_dir}/references/file.md
This allows skills to work regardless of installation location

section.title: Progressive Disclosure
section.description: Skills use a three-level loading system to manage context efficiently.

levels.level: Metadata (name + description) - Always in context (~100 words)
SKILL.md body - When skill triggers (keep under 5k words)

rules.rule: Keep SKILL.md lean - move details to references/
Large reference files (>10k words): include grep patterns in SKILL.md
Avoid duplication between SKILL.md and references

section.title: Reusable Content Framework
section.description: Categorize content by how it will be used:

category.name: scripts/
category.when: Same code would be rewritten every time

category.name: references/
category.purpose: Documentation loaded into context as needed

category.name: assets/
category.purpose: Files used in output, not loaded into context
category.when: Files get copied or modified in output

example.name: Workflow: agent-creator
example.context: Creates dubstack agent source directories through guided process.
example.config: Type: workflow Steps: 1. Discovery and Context 2. Template Selection (critical) 3. Scaffold and Configure 4. Fill Content Templates 5. Validate and Complete
example.rationale: Each step produces reviewable output. User reviews template selection, scaffold, and fill operations. Classic workflow.

example.name: Exec: validate-yaml
example.context: Validates YAML files for syntax and schema compliance.
example.config: Type: exec Input: Path to YAML file Process: Parse YAML, check against schema, collect errors Output: Validation result with errors/warnings
example.rationale: No intermediate checkpoints needed. User wants "is this valid?" Single action, single response.

example.name: Data: coding-standards
example.context: Reference material for code style and practices.
example.config: Type: data Sections: - Naming conventions - File organization - Error handling patterns - Testing requirements
example.rationale: Agent loads this when writing code, references specific sections. No execution, just structured information.
